segments.text: Hello, my name is Gus Quaid. I am a senior technical specialist for Fusion Data and Process and Autodesk's PLM.
I'm here to present from integration to orchestration using MCP to let AI agents connect PLM and NES.

Today, I will be walking you through how to connect Fusion Manage and Fusion Operations, but really this could apply to Fusion Manage in any downstream system and how we connect them with AI agents via MCP.
The whole premise of this presentation is going to be about how integrations move data, but AI orchestration understands and acts on it.
Let's hop in.
Let me talk about the inspiration for this presentation.
I did a presentation at Autodesk University two years ago about integrating PLM and NES.
These were hard-coded integrations, and they really wanted to prove a point that you don't have to pass through something like ERK,

like a company typically expects, but rather there's some meaningful integrations between your system of engagement for your product definition and process management, and for your decision planning and execution and tracking the decisions you make in manufacturing.
Now, all of this, like this entire presentation, was inspired by this meme on LinkedIn.

I saw this meme, and I really thought to myself, you know, NES doesn't have to go to ERP to get all of its data. That's the typical tech stack, but maybe there are some meaningful integrations with PLM.
So I've taken this idea, and I wanted to augment it with AI, and enabled by MCP.

And so today it looks a little bit more like this, but really it isn't NES looking to MCP to get the data at once, and PLM and ERP there being jealous, but rather every system in the enterprise can benefit from AI orchestration via MCP model context protocol.
So here's the pitch to you. So hopefully if you're in the room, you're going to connect with this and you're going to find it interesting.
I want to ask you the question, your systems are connected, but are they intelligent?
Integrations move data, but they cannot reason across it. So I can have a hard coded integration, but when I hit an edge case, what happens? Probably just failure.
Your systems hold the answers.
And one can ask the right questions and integration can't ask a question. It just passes data and it has answers there.
But who's supposed to ask the question?
Your team bridges gaps manually every day and at scale.

So we're integrations fall short. If you have them even, you find that people are having to piece together data, playing the role of kind of a detective to figure out what happened and what was the reason and what's going on in the business.
What if the systems you already have could do more without replacing what works. So without replacing those integrations or without replacing any of the systems that have pulled the answers.
So this is where MCP comes in. So we have rigid systems, but it is a messy reality.

Let's talk through some of the messy realities on structured data across emails, PDFs and notes, human variability. So you have operator judgment, you have shift handoffs, you have tribal knowledge that might be invisible to MES and PLM.
So across domain reasoning, you have design changes that maybe get documented and reasons through in PLM, but need to somehow flow downstream to other systems like MES ERP and your supply chain.

So this is an unprotectable exception. So an integration platform, you know, integrating with APIs handles maybe 80% of use cases, but there's the edge cases. There's the things that require flexibility and creativity.
It's the hard part and this isn't something that lands in an integration. It's something that lands in someone's inbox and waits and needs to be dealt with manually.
So did integrations do have their place though? Let me talk about that. What they do well, they're reliable, they're deterministic in how they move data at scale.
And they're auditable, they're predefined and their production hearted. You know exactly what's going to happen. You can see what happened. You can see where successes and failures are happening.

But let's talk about where they stop. Your integrations can't reason they brought data. And every new question needs a new integration. So by new question every single new way of moving data around requires a new hard coded integration or like I just said, it lands in someone's inbox and waits for manual intervention.
Model context protocol is the bridge that handles the places where integrations fall short.

So if you're someone that's been keeping up with model context protocol, keeping up with agentic AI, you'll probably breeze right through this, but for those of you who are new to it, let's talk about it.

So I have an AI agent, you know, this is an LOM, but it's predisposition for not dealing with tasks, but rather goals. This is something that can have a goal to analyze or kind of be a system layer that I can talk to and understand.
It can be something that's always on as well, always waiting to act on data.

MCP is a layer that sits on top of the AI agent or you could say between the MCP layer contains tools, resources, prompts and context that an AI agent can use an agent won't directly call an API, but rather it uses things like the tools to be able to act upon that API.
We get the raw response from the API, but rather it gets a processed response from the tool that exists inside the MCP server inside that MCP layer.
And then we talked to other systems, PLM and MES being the ones we're focusing on while and QMS as well inside of this demonstration and presentation, but also ERP IoT systems supplier systems.

This is inter replacement for integrations. It operates on top of existing integrations. This is all composable by design. So it can interface with any tool, any system without some sort of custom point to point logic.

And it's context aware. So the system you may have configured the system you want to bring online, the agent will be aware of that system. So it isn't really going in blind. It's not operating in a black box.
But rather it knows all the systems it's working with and has tailored tools to interact with them.
